messages.content:
@echo off
color 0a

set "PASSWORD=yellowstar520"

:LOGIN
cls
echo Enter password to access Project Terminal BETA:
set /p "USERPASS=>"
if "%USERPASS%"=="%PASSWORD%" goto MENU
echo Incorrect password. Try again.
timeout /t 2 /nobreak > NUL
goto LOGIN

:MENU
cls
echo *** Project Terminal BETA ***
echo.
echo 1. Command
echo 2. Proxy
echo 3. Restart
echo 4. Exit
echo.
set /p option=Choose an option:

if "%option%"=="1" goto COMMAND
if "%option%"=="2" start https://navy.strangled.net
if "%option%"=="3" goto RESTART
if "%option%"=="4" exit
goto MENU

:COMMAND
cls
echo *** EXECUTE COMMANDS ***
echo.
set /p "command=> "
echo.

:LOOP
if "%command%"=="end" goto MENU
echo Running command: %command%
echo.
cmd /c "%command%"
echo.
set /p "command=> "
goto LOOP

:RESTART
cls
echo Restarting the program...
echo.
timeout /t 2 /nobreak > NUL
call "%~f0"
goto MENU
I'm sorry, as an AI language model I cannot execute programs or run code. Is there anything else I can assist you with?
Can you make a script in batch that runs raw code from a website, the code is in python.

Yes, it's possible to download and execute code from a website using a batch script. However, it's important to ensure that the code from the website is safe and secure before executing it on your computer. Here's an example script that downloads and executes a Python script from a website:

```
@echo off
setlocal

REM URL of Python script to download and execute
set "SCRIPT_URL=https://example.com/script.py"

REM Download the Python script and save it to a temporary file
set "TMP_FILE=%TEMP%\code.py"
powershell -command "(New-Object System.Net.WebClient).DownloadFile('%SCRIPT_URL%', '%TMP_FILE%')"

REM Execute the Python script using the Python executable
set "PYTHON_EXE=%SYSTEMROOT%\System32\Python.exe"
if not exist "%PYTHON_EXE%" (
    echo Error: Python is not installed on this computer.
    goto :EOF
)
"%PYTHON_EXE%" "%TMP_FILE%"

REM Delete temporary file
del "%TMP_FILE%"

endlocal
```

In this example, the script uses PowerShell to download the Python code from the specified website and save it to a temporary file. Then, it uses the Python executable to execute the downloaded code. Finally, it deletes the temporary file.

Note that this script assumes Python is installed on the computer and that the URL for the script is secure and trustworthy. It's important to carefully review any code that you download from the internet before executing it on your computer.
It apppears to have just opened the console and showed red text for a split second, then exited.

I apologize for the inconvenience. Here's an updated version of the script that should work better:

```
@echo off
setlocal

REM URL of Python script to download and execute
set "SCRIPT_URL=https://example.com/script.py"

REM Download the Python script and save it to a temporary file

powershell -Command "(New-Object System.Net.WebClient).DownloadFile('%SCRIPT_URL%', '%TMP_FILE%')"
```

Make sure to replace "https://example.com/script.py" with the actual URL of the Python script you want to download and run. Also, make sure that the Python script is compatible with your version of Python and that it doesn't contain any malicious code.
Is there one that won't run through powershell?

Sure, here's an updated version of the script that doesn't use PowerShell.

```
@echo off
setlocal

REM URL of Python script to download and execute
set "SCRIPT_URL=https://example.com/script.py"

REM Download the Python script and save it to a temporary file
set "TMP_FILE=%TEMP%\code.py"
certutil -urlcache -split -f "%SCRIPT_URL%" "%TMP_FILE%" > nul

REM Execute the Python script using the Python executable
set "PYTHON_EXE=%SYSTEMROOT%\System32\Python.exe"
if not exist "%PYTHON_EXE%" (
    echo Error: Python is not installed on this computer.
    goto :EOF
)
"%PYTHON_EXE%" "%TMP_FILE%"

REM Delete temporary file
del "%TMP_FILE%"

endlocal
```

This version uses certutil to download the file and save it to the temporary file location specified by the `%TEMP%` environment variable. Then, it executes the Python script using the Python executable. Finally, it deletes the temporary file.

As before, make sure to replace the "https://example.com/script.py" URL with the actual URL of the Python script you want to download and run. And make sure that the Python script is compatible with your version of Python and that it doesn't contain any malicious code.
Is it possible for it to not go through the command prompt either?

If you don't want to use the command prompt, you can download and run the Python script using a VBScript or JScript file. Here's an example VBScript file:

```vbscript
Set objXMLHttp = CreateObject("MSXML2.XMLHTTP")
objXMLHttp.open "GET", "https://example.com/script.py", False
objXMLHttp.send

If objXMLHttp.Status = 200 Then
    Set objFSO = CreateObject("Scripting.FileSystemObject")
    strFile = objFSO.GetSpecialFolder(2) & "\temp\script.py"
    
    Set objFile = objFSO.CreateTextFile(strFile, True)
    objFile.Write(objXMLHttp.responseText)
    objFile.Close
    
    Set objShell = CreateObject("WScript.Shell")
    objShell Run "%SYSTEMROOT%\System32\Python.exe " & strFile, 1, True
    
    objFSO.DeleteFile(strFile)
Else
    WScript.Echo "Failed: " & objXMLHttp.Status
End If
```

This VBScript file downloads the Python script from the specified URL and saves it to a temporary file. Then it runs the Python script using the Python executable and waits for it to finish. Finally, it deletes the temporary file.

To use this VBScript file, open Notepad, paste the script, save it with a .vbs extension, and double-click it to run. Note that you'll need to replace "https://example.com/script.py" with the actual URL of the Python script you want to download and run. And make sure that the Python script is compatible with your version of Python and that it doesn't contain any malicious code.
Would it delete the file that was downloaded after you exit the program?

Yes, the VBScript file I provided would delete the file that was downloaded after the Python script completes. 

```
objFSO.DeleteFile(strFile)
```

This line deletes the temporary file after running the Python script. The `objFSO` object in this line is an instance of the `Scripting.FileSystemObject` class, which provides methods for working with files and folders. The `objFSO.DeleteFile` method is used to delete the file specified in the `strFile` variable.

To ensure that the temporary file is deleted even if the Python script terminates unexpectedly, you can modify the VBScript to delete the file at the beginning of the script as well. Here's the modified script:

```vbscript
Set objFSO = CreateObject("Scripting.FileSystemObject")
strFile = objFSO.GetSpecialFolder(2) & "\temp\script.py"

' Delete temporary file if it already exists
If objFSO.FileExists(strFile) Then
    objFSO.DeleteFile(strFile)
End If

Set objXMLHttp = CreateObject("MSXML2.XMLHTTP")
objXMLHttp.open "GET", "https://example.com/script.py", False
objXMLHttp.send

If objXMLHttp.Status = 200 Then
    
    Set objFile = objFSO.CreateTextFile(strFile, True)
    objFile.Write(objXMLHttp.responseText)
    objFile.Close
    
    Set objShell = CreateObject("WScript.Shell")
    objShell.Run "%SYSTEMROOT%\System32\Python.exe " & strFile, 1, True
    
    objFSO.DeleteFile(strFile)
Else
    WScript.Echo "Failed: " & objXMLHttp.Status
End If
```

This version of the script checks if the temporary file already exists and deletes it before downloading the new file. That way, you don't end up with multiple versions of the file cluttering up your hard drive.
Line 4 character 1

I apologize for the error in my previous response. It seems that there was a typo in the VBScript that could've caused a syntax error. Here's the corrected VBScript that should work without any errors:

```vbscript
Set objXMLHttp = CreateObject("MSXML2.XMLHTTP")
objXMLHttp.open "GET", "https://example.com/script.py", False
objXMLHttp.send

If objXMLHttp.Status = 200 Then
    Set objFSO = CreateObject("Scripting.FileSystemObject")
    strFile = objFSO.GetSpecialFolder(2) & "\temp\script.py"
    
    Set objFile = objFSO.CreateTextFile(strFile, True)
    objFile.Write(objXMLHttp.responseText)
    objFile.Close
    
    Set objShell = CreateObject("WScript.Shell")
    objShell.Run "%SYSTEMROOT%\System32\Python.exe " & strFile, 1, True
    
    objFSO.DeleteFile(strFile)
Else
    WScript.Echo "Failed: " & objXMLHttp.Status
End If
```

Make sure to replace "https://example.com/script.py" with the actual URL of the Python script you want to download and run. And make sure that the Python script is compatible with your version of Python and that it doesn't contain any malicious code.
Failed Code: 403

If you're receiving an HTTP 403 error code when attempting to download the Python script using VBScript, it means that the server is denying access to the file. This might be because you don't have the correct permissions to access the file, or because the server is configured to block automated downloads.

In this case, I would recommend reaching out to the owner of the website to request permission to download the Python script. Alternatively, you could manually download the script using a web browser and then run it locally on your computer using the Python interpreter.
It is my stuff on pastebin.com

If you're getting a 403 error code while trying to download a Python script from Pastebin, it could be due to a couple of reasons.

Pastebin could be detecting that you are trying to automate your downloads and is blocking access. In this case, you can try to add a user agent string to the request. A user agent string will tell the server that you are accessing it from a regular web browser, and not programmatically.

Here's an updated version of the VBScript that includes a user agent string:

```vbscript
Set objXMLHttp = CreateObject("MSXML2.XMLHTTP")
objXMLHttp.open "GET", "https://pastebin.com/raw/code.py", False
objXMLHttp.setRequestHeader "User-Agent", "Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/58.0.3029.110 Safari/537.36 Edge/16.16299"
objXMLHttp.send

If objXMLHttp.Status = 200 Then
    Set objFSO = CreateObject("Scripting.FileSystemObject")
    strFile = objFSO.GetSpecialFolder(2) & "\temp\code.py"
    
    Set objFile = objFSO.CreateTextFile(strFile, True)
    objFile.Write(objXMLHttp.responseText)
    objFile.Close
    
    Set objShell = CreateObject("WScript.Shell")
    objShell.Run "%SYSTEMROOT%\System32\Python.exe " & strFile, 1, True
    
    objFSO.DeleteFile(strFile)
Else
    WScript.Echo "Failed: " & objXMLHttp.Status
End If
```

Make sure you change "https://pastebin.com/raw/code.py" to your actual Pastebin raw code URL.

Another reason for the 403 error could be that your Python script contains sensitive information, such as API keys. Pastebin might have detected that, and is prohibiting access for security reasons.
Just to double check, this is python, right?

Yes, the VBScript code I provided is intended to download and execute Python code. 

In the VBScript code, the following line specifies the Python executable and the downloaded Python script file to run:

```vbscript
objShell.Run "%SYSTEMROOT%\System32\Python.exe " & strFile, 1, True
```

Here, `%SYSTEMROOT%\System32\Python.exe` is the path to the Python executable, and `strFile` is the path to the downloaded Python script file.

Make sure that the Python code you're trying to download and run is indeed written in Python and contains valid Python syntax.

This is the beginning of a batch script that requires a password to access the Project Terminal BETA. It sets the password as "yellowstar520" in the line:

```
set "PASSWORD=yellowstar520"
```

The `:LOGIN` label starts a loop that will keep prompting the user to enter the correct password until it is entered. Here's the rest of the script:

```
set /p "USERPASS=>"
if "%USERPASS%"=="%PASSWORD%" goto MENU
echo Incorrect password. Try again.
timeout /t 2 /nobreak > NUL
goto LOGIN

:MENU
cls
echo *** Project Terminal BETA ***
echo.
echo 1. Command
echo 2. Proxy
echo 3. Restart
echo 4. Exit
echo.
set /p option=Choose an option:

if "%option%"=="1" goto COMMAND
if "%option%"=="2" start https://navy.strangled.net
if "%option%"=="3" goto RESTART
if "%option%"=="4" exit
goto MENU

:COMMAND
cls
echo *** EXECUTE COMMANDS ***
echo.
set /p "command=> "
echo.

:LOOP
if "%command%"=="end" goto MENU
echo Running command: %command%
echo.
cmd /c "%command%"
echo.
set /p "command=> "
goto LOOP

:RESTART
cls
echo Restarting the program...
echo.
timeout /t 2 /nobreak > NUL
call "%~f0"
goto MENU
```

Once the user enters the correct password, it goes to the `:MENU` label, which displays a menu of options. Depending on the option the user chooses, the script goes to a different label and executes a certain code. 

For example, if the user enters "1", it goes to `:COMMAND`, which prompts the user to enter a command. The script then enters a loop that runs the entered command until the user enters "end". 

If the user enters "2", it opens a URL using the start command.

If the user enters "3", the script restarts the program.

If the user enters "4", it exits the program.

Note that this script is just an example and may require modification to fit your specific needs. It's important to note that storing passwords in plain text like this can be insecure. You might consider using a more secure method for storing passwords.
Is it written in Python?

No, this appears to be a batch script (also known as a Windows Command Script) that runs on Windows operating system, not a Python script. 

The script uses Windows batch commands (`set`, `echo`, `if`, `goto`, `call`, etc.) to prompt the user for input, verify a password, display a menu of options, and execute various commands based on user input. It's a convenient way to automate tasks on a Windows computer.
Can you convert the script to python?

Yes, I can convert the batch script to Python code that performs similar functionality. Here is the equivalent Python code:

```python
import getpass
import os

# Define the password to access the program
PASSWORD = "yellowstar520"

# Prompt the user to enter the password
while True:
    userpass = getpass.getpass("Enter password to access Project Terminal BETA: ")
    if userpass == PASSWORD:
        break
    else:
        print("Incorrect password. Try again.")
        input("Press Enter to continue...")
        os.system('cls')

# Display the menu of options
while True:
    os.system('cls')
    print("*** Project Terminal BETA ***")
    print()
    print("1. Command")
    print("2. Proxy")
    print("3. Restart")
    print("4. Exit")
    print()
    
    # Prompt the user to choose an option
    option = input("Choose an option: ")
    
    # Execute the option chosen by the user
    if option == "1":
        while True:
            os.system('cls')
            print("*** EXECUTE COMMANDS ***")
            print()
            command = input("Enter command: ")
            if command == "end":
                break
            os.system(command)
            input("Press Enter to continue...")
    elif option == "2":
        os.system('start https://navy.strangled.net')
        input("Press Enter to continue...")
    elif option == "3":
        os.system('cls')
        print("Restarting...")
        input("Press Enter to continue...")
        os.system('cls')
        os.system('python "' + __file__ + '"')
        break
    elif option == "4":
        break
    else:
        print("Invalid option. Try again.")
        input("Press Enter to continue...")
```

This Python code uses `getpass` module to prompt the user to enter the password. It then displays a menu of options using Python's built-in `print()` function. It takes the user's option choice using Python's built-in `input()` function. Based on the option the user has chosen, it executes specific functionality or displays error/
